course_description:
This course introduces students to First Nations health and wellbeing, with a particular focus on Global Indigenous Peoples and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities. It foregrounds Indigenous voices, scholarship, and cultures in both global and Australian contexts, encouraging students to critically examine historical and contemporary determinants of health that contribute to racial health inequities.

Grounded in public health, the course adopts participatory, collaborative, strengths-based, and decolonising approaches. Through theory and case studies, students will explore key concepts and frameworks that support culturally safe and respectful engagement with First Nations communities.

This course encourages interdisciplinary and reflexive engagement, inviting students to engage in critical self-reflection, examining their own cultural values and privileges in cross-cultural interactions. This process aims to foster culturally safe practices and deepen understanding of allyship and solidarity with First Nations Peoples, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples.

Aligned with the theme of National Reconciliation Week 2025, Bridging Now to Next , this course encourages students to join a collective reconciliation effort, stepping forward together, guided by the lessons of the past, and committed to shaping a more equitable future.

A really powerful aspect of this course will be the incredible speakers that you will meet either in-person or online. We have invited Indigenous and non-Indigenous leaders - academics, researchers, community members, health professionals and organisational representatives from Australia and Global, who create change at a human rights level, those working to provide a culturally-appropriate design to spaces and clinics, people working on-ground to improve health behaviours, and many more. Their stories will be shared with you in a way that highlights the strengths of First Nations Peoples. You will also have the benefit of a visit from our Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander tutors, community members and our ‘Elder in residence’ to explain, teach and share with you.

Additional timetable information
Whether your course is delivered internally (weekly lectorials and tutorials at Herston campus) or externally, all course information can be found on the course Blackboard site.
To log on to Blackboard, click here:
https://www.elearning.uq.edu.au/
course_aims: The overall aim of this course is to equip students with a critical understanding of the health and wellbeing of First Nations Peoples in both contemporary Australian and global contexts. This knowledge is intended to inform and enhance students’ professional roles and practices, fostering culturally safe, respectful, and effective engagement with Indigenous communities.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand and apply a culturally safe practice.
Describe First Nations Health in a historical context and analyse the impact of colonial processes on First Nation health outcomes.
Apply and critically assess the role of the determinants of health to global and national First Nations health outcomes.
Apply a health equity framework to analyse the strengths of First Nations led health initiatives.
Apply participatory and collaborative approaches to create sustainable, culturally safe and responsive health policy, services, and systems.
Demonstrate skills in written and oral communication, advocacy, reflection, digital capabilities, and teamwork.

assessment_details.title: Critical Reflexivity Portfolio Through Decolonising Lens
assessment_details.weight: 45% VLog 1 is 15%; VLog 2 is 15%; VLog 3 is 15%
assessment_details.due_date: VLog 1 (A Personal Standpoint) 21/08/2025 2:00 pm VLog 2 (Culturally Safe Practice) 11/09/2025 2:00 pm VLog 3 (Advocacy and Health Systems) 9/10/2025 2:00 pm
assessment_details.task_description: A Reflexive Portfolio is a digital collection of your reflexive inquiries into big questions and themes prompted through the Semester in INDH7002. These questions or themes will be weaved through the interactive Modules, Lectorials, Tutorials and Readings. The Portfolio gives students the personal space to dive deep into their own reflexive processes using decolonising lens. Developing a strong reflexive practice is foundational in being a respectful, culturally sensitive, equitable, and well-informed practitioner, scholar and Ally. Reflexivity is vital to understanding how cultural paradigms and our own experiences influence our worldviews, unconscious biases, and philosophy in healthcare and research. This Portfolio involves three discrete video logs (VLogs). You are asked to complete a Digital Module (each extends on lectorials, reading materials & tutorial content) for each submission in the Portfolio. The Modules will provide you the space to reflect on, in and for culturally safe practice and engage with broader social and political issues impacting health outcomes. Integrating peer-reviewed and grey literature is required for each submission to your Reflexive Portfolio. Module 1 Personal Standpoint Reflexive Vlog Submission Due Week 4 (15%) Module 2 Culturally Safe Practice Reflexive Vlog Submission - Due Week 7 (15%) Module 3 Advocacy and Health Systems Reflexive Vlog Submission - Due Week 10 (15%) Learning Objectives Assessed: 1, 2, 3, 6 Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
All assessment items are conducted via the ULTRA Blackboard site. This includes all Undergraduate and Postgraduate (internal and external) courses. Instructions are provided for each assessment item on each course site.
Follow the instructions regarding Assessment submission on your Blackboard site. Instructions will vary from course to course, and in some cases, instructions will vary for different assessment items within a course. For example, you may be asked to label submissions in a particular way, or you may be asked to submit via a TurnItIn link or use a cover sheet. If you are instructed to submit a cover sheet, complete each section carefully, with the correct course name and number, assignment number and title, and your Course Coordinator's name. Typing your name in the Declaration will suffice as your signature that the work has not been plagiarised. An electronic copy of the School of Public Health assignment cover page, including AI Statement is available on each Blackboard site. However, some course coordinators include a cover sheet in a template document that they wish you to submit. The most important thing is that you should make yourself aware of the requirements for assessment submission for each course item and follow the instructions completely. If in doubt, ask your tutor or Course Coordinator. Further information on University policies and procedures is available under Manage my program at https://my.uq.edu.au/ .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
To apply for an extension for a piece of progressive assessment (eg assignments, oral presentations and computer based assignments) students should complete the online request at https://my.uq.edu.au/node/218/1 . Please note that your request may be refused if you do not meet the acceptable reasons for an extension. If requesting an extension on medical grounds, a medical certificate must be provided. The extension will be approved for the number of days included in the medical certificate that the student was not fit to study or work, eg if the medical certificate is for 3 days, an extension will be approved for 3 days maximum regardless of the student's request. If requesting an extension using a Student Access Plan for Disability (SAPD) as evidence, a maximum of 7-day extension will be approved in the first instance. Updated medical documentation, as well as a copy of the SAPD, is required if requesting an extension for more than 7 days. The following SPH guidelines are consistent with the UQ policy. However, the SPH Guidelines contain specific rules and interpretations for SPH courses, and applications for extension and penalties for late submissions will be judged according to the guidelines outlined in this document. You should read both the information in your my.UQ at the following link: https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-extension?p=2#2 and the SPH guidelines (below) before submitting a request for an extension. The SPH Guidelines apply to all courses offered by the School of Public Health unless the Course Profile explicitly states otherwise.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.You should submit a request for an extension as soon as it becomes clear you need an extension. Your request should be submitted no later than the assessment item's due date and time.

assessment_details.title: Quiz
assessment_details.task_description: This quiz is designed to assess students’ understanding of key concepts introduced in INDH7002, with a focus on First Nations health and wellbeing in both Australian and global contexts. The quiz will consist of multiple-choice questions (MCQs) that evaluate students’ ability to engage with course content, including Indigenous perspectives, cultural safety, historical and contemporary determinants of health and decolonising approaches to public health. The aim is to consolidate foundational knowledge to topic areas covered in the course including: Indigenous global perspectives Social determinants of health, health inequities and the impact of colonisation Culturally safe and respectful engagement Reflexivity and standpoint Health systems and policy Community controlled services and public health practice Public health frameworks grounded in participatory and strengths-based approaches The quiz will consist of Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs). Full details, including instructions and access information, will be provided on Blackboard Ultra prior to the assessment week. Learning Objectives Assessed: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Students must complete the quiz by the set due date. Extensions for Assessment 2 (Quiz 2) will not be granted except under exceptional circumstances.
If you believe you have grounds for an extension, you must speak with the course coordinator before submitting a request. Please note that extension requests for this quiz are generally not considered, so timely completion is essential.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
As the quiz is set within a specific date range, students will not be able to apply for an extension for assessment 2, unless it is under exceptional circumstances. However, students will need to speak with the course coordinator before applying for an extension for Quiz 2, as this will not be considered.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Please refer to 10. Policies & Guidelines

assessment_details.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.title: Health Equity Pitch
assessment_details.task_description: As a public health consultant, you have been invited to work with a 'mainstream’ (non-Indigenous run/led) organisation. The organisation has heard of the newly launched ‘Making Tracks Together’ Queensland’s Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Health Equity Framework and have invited you to meet with the leaders of the organisation. You have been asked to showcase the potential value and opportunities of the Health Equity Framework for this organisation. Purpose: Your task will be to pitch the value of the Health Equity Framework via an oral presentation to the leaders of an organisation committed to health equity for First Nations Peoples. This assessment encourages you to creatively and critically communicate the relevance and impact of the Health Equity Framework. You should demonstrate how the framework supports culturally safe, respectful, and effective engagement with First Nations communities and contributes to addressing racial health inequities. Modality: This is a recorded presentation with your camera (You may use any medium such as PowerPoint slides or a digital poster or a graphics recording) which reflects Indigenous ways of doing. Your role: Public Health Consultant Audience: A non-Indigenous organisation (of your choosing) who are committed to the (chosen) health equity issue (Can be international or local) Conditions: Oral pitch time limit: 8 minutes (+/-10mins, excludes Acknowledgement of Country) Recorded presentations tool/s: Choose a tool such as PowerPoint slides, digital poster or visual graphical recording style Referencing style: APA 7th (include a References slide with a minimum of 7 references ) Learning Objectives Assessed: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Week 1:Introduction to First Nations Health and Wellbeing This session will introduce INDH7002, outlining what will be covered over the 13 weeks, including key topic areas covered in the course, assessments, and expectations. Students will gain an overview of the course structure, learning outcomes, and how each assessment supports their understanding of First Nations health and wellbeing in both Australian and global contexts. Sub-activity: Podcast – Decolonising Public Health!

Week 2: Identity & Culture: Indigenous Ways of Knowing, Being and Doing This session explores the foundational concepts of Indigenous identity and culture, focusing on First Nations ways of knowing, being, and doing. Students will be introduced to Indigenous epistemologies and ontologies, which shape how knowledge is understood, lived, and shared within communities.
Week 2: Tutorial 1 - What does it mean to learn on Country (and on stolen lands)?

Week 3: Hidden Histories: First Nations Perspectives on Resistance and Resilience in Pre- and Post- Colonial Context This session uncovers the often-overlooked histories of First Nations resistance and resilience across both pre- and post-colonial periods. Students will engage with Indigenous perspectives that challenge dominant historical narratives, highlighting the strength, agency, and survival of First Nations Peoples in the face of colonisation, dispossession, and systemic injustice. Through storytelling, historical accounts, and critical analysis, the session will explore how resistance has taken many forms—from cultural continuity and land protection to political activism and community leadership. It will also examine the enduring resilience of First Nations communities, grounded in deep cultural knowledge, kinship systems, and spiritual connection to Country. Students will be encouraged to reflect on the importance of truth-telling, historical justice, and the role of education in supporting reconciliation and equity. Sub-activity: Deadly Yarn Podcast – Resistance and resilience of colonisation of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples!

Week 4: Global Indigenous Peoples and Perspectives in Indigenous Health This session explores the diverse experiences and perspectives of Global Indigenous Peoples in relation to health and wellbeing. Students will examine how colonisation, systemic inequities, and cultural resilience shape Indigenous health outcomes across different global contexts, including Aotearoa (New Zealand), Canada, the United States, and the Pacific. The session will highlight shared challenges—such as dispossession, marginalisation, and health disparities—as well as the strength of Indigenous knowledge systems, community-led health initiatives, and cultural revitalisation efforts. Students will be encouraged to reflect on the importance of global solidarity, Indigenous sovereignty, and culturally grounded approaches to health that honour Indigenous ways of knowing, being, and doing.
Week 4: Tutorial 2 - Preparing a standpoint: Introducing Module 1 and reflexivity frameworks through decolonising lens

Week 5: Understanding Determinants in Indigenous Health This session explores the social, cultural, historical, and political determinants that shape the health and wellbeing of First Nations Peoples. Students will critically examine how colonisation, systemic racism, intergenerational trauma, and socio-economic disadvantage contribute to ongoing health inequities. Students will be encouraged to reflect on how these determinants intersect and how health professionals can respond in ways that are culturally safe, respectful, and equity-focused. Sub-activity: Deadly Yarn Podcast - Cultural determinants of health: cultural identity, connection to land, culture and language and self-determination!

Week 6: Rights-Based Approach to Health & Health Equity This session introduces students to a rights-based approach to health, emphasising the importance of human rights, social justice, and equity in addressing health disparities experienced by First Nations Peoples. Students will explore how international and national frameworks—such as the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP)—inform ethical and culturally safe health practices. The session will critically examine how systemic barriers and discriminatory policies have impacted Indigenous health outcomes, and how a rights-based lens can support advocacy, accountability, and community empowerment. Students will also consider the role of health professionals and institutions in upholding the rights of Indigenous Peoples through inclusive, respectful, and equity-driven approaches. Through case studies, students will reflect on how rights-based principles intersect with Indigenous worldviews and contribute to meaningful change in health systems.
Week 6: Tutorial 3 - Introducing Module 2 - Cultural safety & reflexivity

Week 7: Racism as a Structural Determinant of Health This session critically examines racism as a structural determinant of health, with a focus on its impact on First Nations Peoples in Australia and Indigenous communities globally. Students will explore how racism operates across systems—including healthcare, education, housing, and justice—to produce and sustain health inequities. The session will unpack the difference between interpersonal, institutional, and systemic racism and how these intersect with other social determinants. Students will also engage with frameworks that support anti-racist and decolonising approaches in public health practice. By the end of the session, students will be able to identify how racism shapes health outcomes, reflect on their own positionality, and consider strategies for promoting equity and accountability within health systems. Sub-activity: Podcast - Racism and Anti-racism!

Week 8: Health Systems & Policy: Political Determinants of Health This session examines the political determinants of health and their influence on health systems, policies, and outcomes for First Nations Peoples. Students will explore how political ideologies, governance structures, legislation, and resource allocation shape access to healthcare and contribute to systemic inequities. Through a decolonising lens, the session will critically analyse the role of policy-making in perpetuating or challenging health disparities, and highlight the importance of Indigenous leadership, advocacy, and sovereignty in shaping health systems that are culturally safe and responsive. Students will engage with case studies and policy frameworks to understand how political decisions impact health equity and reflect on the responsibilities of health professionals in promoting justice and accountability within public health systems.

Week 8: Tutorial 4 – Introducing Module 3 - Navigating health systems & reflexivity Sub-activity: Deadly Yarn Podcast - Evidence strength-based approach: Community-led programmes to improve Indigenous Health!

Week 9: Health Systems: Indigenous Community Controlled Health Services in Public Health Practice This session explores the role of Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Organisation Services (ACCHOS) and the National Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Organisation(NACHOS: https://www.naccho.org.au/) within the broader Australian health system, emphasising their significance in advancing public health outcomes for First Nations peoples. Students will learn from First Nations voices and scholarship, gaining insight into the principles of self-determination, holistic care, and community empowerment that underpin NACHOS and ACCHOS. By the end of the session, students will be equipped to apply culturally responsive strategies in public health practice and advocate for systems-level change that supports Indigenous-led health initiatives. Sub-activity:
Mid-Semester Break

Week 10: Community Led: Co-design & Leadership This session explores the principles and practices of co-design and leadership in the development and implementation of community-based health programmes, with a particular focus on Indigenous communities. Grounded in a strengths-based approach, the session highlights how Indigenous leadership, cultural knowledge, and community priorities shape effective and sustainable health interventions. Students will examine real-world examples of community-led initiatives that have improved health outcomes by fostering empowerment, resilience, and self-determination. The session emphasises collaborative methods that value Indigenous voices throughout the design, delivery, and evaluation of health services. Through critical reflection and case-based learning, students will develop skills in ethical engagement, culturally safe practice, and systems thinking to support Indigenous-led health innovation and leadership.
Week 10: Tutorial 5 - Preparing for your pitch: co-design and working with First Nations communities

Week 11: Community Advocacy: Implications for Policy and Practice This session examines community advocacy in shaping health policy and practice with a particular focus on Indigenous health equity. Students will explore how grassroots movements, community-led initiatives, and Indigenous leadership influence systemic change and challenge dominant policy frameworks. The session highlights the mechanisms by which advocacy translates into policy reform, improved service delivery, and culturally responsive health systems. Emphasis is placed on the role of public health professionals in supporting advocacy efforts ethically and effectively, while amplifying Indigenous voices and priorities. Students will gain practical insights into policy engagement, coalition-building, and strategic communication, equipping them to contribute meaningfully to advocacy efforts that promote justice, equity, and self-determination in health. Sub-activity: Podcast - Allyship!

Week 12: Reconciliation and Working as an Ally in Solidarity for First Nations Health Equity This session focuses on the role of non-Indigenous allies in advancing health equity for First Nations peoples through respectful, informed, and sustained solidarity. It explores the principles of reconciliation, allyship, and ethical engagement, emphasising how allies can support Indigenous-led health initiatives. Students will critically examine the historical and systemic factors contributing to health disparities and learn how to engage in culturally safe practices that centre Indigenous voices, leadership, and self-determination. The session highlights the importance of humility, accountability, and ongoing learning in allyship and provides practical strategies for working collaboratively within health systems to promote equity and justice.
Week 12: Tutorial 6 - Preparing your pitch

Week 13: Reflection on Your Learning Journey: Upbeat Q & A Discussion This final session offers a dynamic and engaging space for students to reflect on their personal and collective learning journey throughout the course. The session encourages open dialogue, storytelling, and shared insights, fostering a sense of closure and celebration. Students will have the opportunity to ask questions, share reflections, and explore how their learning can be applied in future public health practice. Emphasis will be placed on growth, transformation, and continuing the journey of respectful engagement and advocacy for First Nations health equity. Additionally, students will be invited to share their feedback through the course evaluation (SECaT), contributing to the continuous improvement of the learning experience. At the end of this course, we hope students feel inspired, connected, and confident in their role as culturally responsive public health practitioners

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines School of Public Health (SPH) Guidelines for late submission of progressive assessment - Preamble To apply for an extension to the due date for a piece of progressive assessment (eg assignments, oral presentations and computer-based assignments) students should complete the online request at https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-assessment-extension?p=2#2 Information regarding deferral of in-semester exams and end-of-semester exams is available from https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/deferring-exam If requesting an extension on medical grounds, a medical certificate must be provided. The extension will be approved for the number of days included in the medical certificate that the student was not fit to study or work, eg if the medical certificate is for 3 days, an extension will be approved for 3 days maximum regardless of the student's request. If requesting an extension using a Student Access Plan (SAP) as evidence, a maximum of 7-day extension will be approved in the first instance. Updated medical documentation, as well as a copy of the SAP, is required if requesting an extension for more than 7 days. A student may have a maximum of three extension requests approved for a single assessment task. An extension for an assessment item due within the study period in which the course is offered, must not exceed four weeks in total. The following SPH guidelines are consistent with the UQ policy. However, the SPH Guidelines contain specific rules and interpretations for SPH courses, and requests for extension and penalties for late submissions will be judged according to the guidelines outlined in this document. You should read both the information in your my.UQ at the following link: https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-assessment-extension and the SPH guidelines (below) before submitting a request for an extension. The SPH Guidelines apply to all courses offered by the School of Public Health unless the Course Profile explicitly states otherwise. SPH Guidelines for late submission of progressive assessment Initial extension for an individual item of assessment – the SPH Student & Academic Administration Team and/or the Course Coordinator decides. This could be for medical or compassionate reasons, or if, in the opinion of the Course Coordinator, there are exceptional circumstances. Acceptable and unacceptable reasons for an extension are listed at the following link, along with the required evidence to be provided: https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-assessment-extension?p=1#1 A request for an extension to an assessment due date must be submitted through mylUQ as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than one calendar day after the assessment task submission due date and time. If applying for an extension after the due date and time of the assessment item , your request may not be approved. An explanation as to why your request was not submitted prior must be included. If approved, a new due date will be set. This would generally be no later than 7 days after the original due date, however this can be modified to take account of the circumstances of the request and the time that would have been lost from studies. If the new due date is past the date for submission of end-of-semester results, the student will receive an INC (incomplete) result. Second and all subsequent extensions for an individual item of assessment – the SPH Student & Academic Administration Team and/or the Program Convenor together with the Course Coordinator decides. This would only be approved for exceptional circumstance with supporting documentation. Online requests must be made at least 24 hours prior to the due date from the first extension. The SPH Student & Academic Administration Team and/or the Course Coordinator will consult with the Program Convenor, who will make the final decision. If approved, the new due date would generally be no later than 7 days after the first extension due date. The Program Convenor should consider if remedial or other support should be offered to the student. The Program Convenor should provide a report on these matters as needed at SPH Examiners’ Meetings. A student may have a maximum of three extension requests approved for a single assessment task. An extension for an assessment item due within the study period in which the course is offered, must not exceed four weeks in total. Please Note : In order to support course progression, extensions that total more than 14 calendar days from the original due date of an assessment item will only be approved in very exceptional circumstances. These requests are assessed and approved or denied on a case-by-case basis. If you have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 4 weeks in a semester, we advise you to carefully consider whether you are capable of successfully completing your courses this semester. You might need to consider applying for removal of course . Penalty for late submission Submission of assignments, practical reports, workbooks, and other types of written assessments after the due date specified in the Course Profile will receive a penalty. The penalty will be a deduction of 10% RELATIVE PERCENTAGE per day ( 24 hour period or part thereof, including weekends and public holidays) or for work graded on a 1-7 scale, a deduction of one grade per day, e.g. If the original mark is 73%, then 10% relative percentage is 10% of this value, ie 7.3%, The final mark for this assessment item after applying the penalty for 1 day late submission would be 73 -7.3 = 65.7% The same outcome is achieved by multiplying the original score by .9; ie 73 x .9 = 65.7% The penalty for multiple days late is the relative percentage multiplied by the number of days late. A submission that is not made within 10 days of the due date will receive a mark of 0% for that assessment item. Where a student has sought more than one extension, the due date for calculating the penalty will be the due date for the most recently approved extension. Submission of Medical Certificates Students are responsible for ensuring that any medical documentation they submit is authentic and signed by a registered medical practitioner. Such practitioners can be identified via the AHPRA website . Also note that: Not all online medical services are staffed by registered practitioners If the registration status of the practitioner cannot be verified, then an alternative practitioner should be sought Students will be held fully responsible for all documentation they submit, even if done so in ignorance of the practitioner's registration status Medical documentation may be subjected to an audit by the University. SPH Assessment Guidelines The School of Public Health assessment tasks have been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. While students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. Learning activities
